title: Seasons and orbital distance
lesson: Goal: Explain why seasons change. Warm-up: Why is summer hotter? Demonstration: Put a flashlight in the center and move a tilted globe closer to the light for summer and farther away for winter. Define axis, orbit, direct light, and indirect light while students watch. After seven minutes, ask students to label four orbit positions and explain why summer is warm.

planted_risks.label: distance misconception
planted_risks.evidence_marker: move a tilted globe closer to the light for summer

title: Adding unlike fractions
lesson: Goal: Add fractions with unlike denominators. Teacher models 1/2 + 1/3 by adding the top numbers and then adding the bottom numbers to get 2/5. Students copy five similar examples from the board. The teacher defines numerator, denominator, equivalent, common, and simplify during the modeling. Students complete ten independent problems; answers are checked at the end.

planted_risks.label: invalid fraction procedure
planted_risks.evidence_marker: adding the bottom numbers to get 2/5

planted_risks.label: practice without feedback
planted_risks.evidence_marker: answers are checked at the end

title: Where plant mass comes from
lesson: Goal: Explain how plants grow. Ask where a tree's wood comes from. Tell students that roots absorb food and most of the tree's mass from soil. Show a time-lapse of a seedling, define photosynthesis and chlorophyll, then have students draw arrows from soil into the roots. Exit question: Why does a plant need good soil to gain mass?

planted_risks.label: mass from soil misconception
planted_risks.evidence_marker: most of the tree's mass from soil

planted_risks.label: assessment reinforces misconception
planted_risks.evidence_marker: Why does a plant need good soil to gain mass?

title: Industrialization through one account
lesson: Goal: Evaluate the effects of industrialization. Read a factory owner's speech praising efficiency, rising output, and new jobs. The source is presented without author context or date. Students highlight three benefits and write a paragraph explaining why industrialization improved life for workers. The strongest paragraph will be read aloud.

planted_risks.label: conclusion predetermined
planted_risks.evidence_marker: why industrialization improved life for workers

title: Evidence-based paragraph
lesson: Goal: Write an evidence-based paragraph about the narrator. Tell students to write a strong paragraph using good evidence and clear reasoning. Do not show the rubric because it will make the task too easy. Students write silently for twenty minutes and submit their first draft for a grade. Early finishers wait; questions are answered individually at the teacher desk.

planted_risks.label: no formative feedback
planted_risks.evidence_marker: submit their first draft for a grade

title: Force and constant motion
lesson: Goal: Explain the relationship between force and motion. State that a moving object needs a continuous forward force or it will immediately stop. Demonstrate by pushing a book across a rough table. Students list the force that keeps a bicycle moving after the rider stops pedaling. Define inertia only in the final two minutes, then ask students whether objects in space eventually run out of motion.
